text: The surgeon marks the anticipated proximal margin on the soft, pliable bowel, noting the absence of diverticula in this section.
The camera shows evidence of old inflammatory disease in the pelvic region, with visible signs that are not severe.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection in the abdominal cavity, carefully separating tissue layers to access the colon.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, carefully retracting tissues towards the midline to expose the ureter.
The surgeon continues dissection over the left kidney, progressing towards the splenic flexure, visible just beyond the ridge.
The surgeon assesses the length of the bowel to ensure a tension-free anastomosis, then proceeds to dissect and take the mesentery attached to the specimen.
The surgeon creates a mesenteric window by carefully dissecting through the mesentery, revealing the underlying structures and preparing for further surgical steps.
The surgeon progresses down towards the upper rectum, navigating through the mesentery to reach the target area.
The surgeon visually assesses the planned margin for resection, examining the area to ensure appropriate boundaries for the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon cleans the margin of the mesenteric window, removing any residual tissue or debris to ensure a clear surgical field.
The surgeon confirms the blood supply to the tissue and proceeds to transect the margin using surgical scissors.
The surgeon prepares to divide the rectosigmoid junction, focusing on the area where the rectum and sigmoid colon meet.
The surgeon morselizes the specimen into smaller pieces, preparing it for extraction.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to prevent oozing of trapped blood within the specimen during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal the vessels, preventing leakage and ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon extracts each smaller piece of the specimen after morseling, following the division of the rectus sigmoid.
The surgeon continues to cut the specimen into smaller pieces, using a technique that ensures the vessels are sealed to prevent blood leakage.
The surgeon extracts the last piece of the specimen using the vessel sealer, ensuring the vessels are sealed to prevent blood leakage.
The surgeon begins sewing an end-to-end anastomosis at the posterior midline, preparing to connect the two bowel ends.
The surgeon begins sewing the end-to-end anastomosis on the patient's left side, taking large, full-thickness bites of the posterior wall tissue.
The surgeon resuspends the uterus, which had dropped into the surgical field, to improve visualization during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon focuses on the lateral aspect of the end-to-end anastomosis, ensuring precise alignment and secure suturing of the bowel segments.
The surgeon observes the healthy edges of the bowel, which appear well-vascularized and intact, suitable for anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares to suture over the anterior row of the bowel anastomosis, beginning the canalization process.
The surgeon places the second suture starting at the posterior midline, adjacent to the first suture, and continues suturing towards the patient's right side.
The surgeon aligns the mucosa of the bowel ends precisely, ensuring a smooth and accurate connection for the anastomosis.
The surgeon checks the perfusion of the bowel by inspecting the open lumens, ensuring adequate blood flow and viability of the tissue.
The surgeon begins the anterior canal suture, extending it across to connect with the original stitch on the left side of the bowel.
The surgeon brings the suture back towards the midline, avoiding knot tying at the corners of the anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the right lower quadrant trocar site using a suture passer, as it is positioned below the arcuate line and at risk for hernia formation.
The surgeon cuts the original suture and ties two ends together, completing the suture knot.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 silk suture to oversew an area where the knot did not come together satisfactorily.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the newly sutured section of the colon, checking for any signs of fluid leakage from the anastomosis site.
The surgeon advances the scope through the anastomosis, providing a clear view of the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis, observing no bubbles indicating a successful seal.
The surgeon places a wad of omentum into the pelvis to cover the anastomosis site.
